title: 2024/12/05 - FD [Part 3]
displayTitle: 2024-12-05 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - Item of business [Part 3]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
description: Thursday 5 December 2024 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Item of business: 2023/24 Annual review of the New Zealand Customs Service Hearing of evidence: Hon Casey Costello, Minister of Customs New Zealand Customs Service Christine Stevenson, Comptroller of Customs Jamie Bamford, Deputy Chief Executive Operations Richard Bargh, Deputy Chief Executive Policy, Legal and Strategy Sharon May, Deputy Chief Executive Enterprise Services Joe Cannon, Deputy Chief Executive International and Governance David Soper, Chief Legal Advisor Greg Hanlen, Chief Financial Officer
descriptionSummary: Item of business. 2023/24 Annual review of the New Zealand Customs Service. Hon Casey Costello, Minister of Customs

chunks.text: Appreciate your time this afternoon. We have half an hour sit down, welcome some introductory comments and you know, questions from members of the committee. Brilliant. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And I would like to say some introductory comments, just, um, to recognize the hard worker customs. Very happy to discuss customs work and protect new and, and protecting New Zealand and to support the efficient and safe flow of trade and travelers.

Um, but a few comments that I'd like to specifically bring to your attention targeting transnational organized crime remains a top priority for me, both as minister of customs and as associate minister of Police. Unfortunately, as you'll be aware, this is an area of growing concern, but customs and its network of domestic and international partner agencies, especially New Zealand police are doing a great job.

As one example, earlier this year in March, 2024, customs and Police helped United States law enforcement prevent almost 200 kilograms of methamphetamine arriving in New Zealand and Australia. This helped dismantle a US based criminal network. So far this year, customs overseas liaison and partner networks have stopped more than two tons of drugs before it reached our border.

In addition, supported through intelligence and targeting customs, frontline offices seized more than three tons of illegal drugs at our border. Customs overseas liaison offices across the Pacific Asia and the Atlantic play a valuable and strategic role in this collaborative work. These offices are also key support for New Zealand's trade interests.

As an example of that support, a few months ago in July, customs liaison officer in Beijing helped address delays in to 8.5 million worth of exports held up at Tja, um, port, which included time critical perishable goods. This is a very small part of the trade customs facilitates, and of course it also collects significant revenue, 17.4 billion last year.

To conclude, I would really sincerely want to acknowledge custom staff and its leadership. The organization has a strong reputation both in New Zealand and overseas as a highly professional agency that operates with integrity, agility, and diligence every day. Customs do a critical work, custom staff do critical work, and they do it to a very high standard, and I thank them for that.

And with that, I'm happy to take questions. Excellent. Thank you very much honor. Thank you, minister. It's good to see you. And, um, like you, I'd like to commend customs and all of it staff members because you're right, they do do a really brilliant job of protecting, um, our board.

My question is about revenue though, um, minister, because as you know, customs is the organization that collects, I think, the most revenue, um, for the country. So we've had a revenue decline of 1.2 billion this year collected by customs compared to the last fiscal year. And I just wanted to know what specific factors contributed to the 6% decrease in the Crown revenue collected by customs in 20 23, 24 compared to the previous financial year, especially in light of, I I remember one of, um, the stated goals that you had from a previous session was

that you wanted revenue growth, um, through increased trade. Yeah. And that, that is a, a big component. We have a clear, I mean, the two priorities I set for customers was to support the government's priority of doubling our exports over the next 10 years. And then the other component is around the organized crime statistics.

So revenue growth is important, but of course, revenue growth is impacted by our economy and, and the, the state of our trading. But in terms of specific and around areas where we've seen that, um, reduction, I think of turn to the comptroller and any specific information. So, uh, it's, it's fairly straightforward.

There have been fewer imports, export, and we collect on those. And when those are down, there is less revenue for the Crown. And we are the second largest collector of revenue after in the revenue. I do want to know, though, just through the details. So was it in general because of, of reduced, uh, trade global economic conditions or domestic policy changes?

What areas, um, specifically did you see this reduction? It's pretty much across the board, so I wouldn't say it, you know, the, the New Zealand economy has been in recession. I think that's pretty, pretty well understood. Uh, we have been, I importing this, we've been exporting this. If I can ask another, uh, specific question.

Was the 1.2 billion, uh, revenue short form more pronounced in specific sectors as you know, as you could see, was it fuel, alcohol, tobacco? What, what were those? Or was it just everything? It was Less a higher value goods imported, and it's a gst so it's a smaller take of gst, But this was forecast by treasury, so it is in my treasury forecast.

So do you have any, um, any strategies for more efficient collection of revenue? Would that actually assist in collecting more of this revenue or No, only because so few of this, these goods not coming through. Yeah, I think, I think it is, um, genuinely around, um, you know, definitely a reduction in the high value goods.

And I think that's across machinery and equipment, big, big ticket items that haven't been being bought in. But the other component I think is recognizing that there is a lot of work that customs has done to build and developed, um, the follow up round trade agreements that have been established. So there has been significant work in that space, um, to facilitate those trade agreements getting off the ground.

And I think that's one of the things I really wanted to recognize around the stuff that's sort of unseen by the public in terms of what customs does in terms of facilitating and operationalizing those agreements as they come through. So I'll ask a different question. How does New Zealand's decline in customs revenue compared to other regions or other global countries?

Or are we unique in this decline or do we actually compare to other OECD countries? Is every other country declining in terms of the revenue that they're collecting? Do You? To be honest, I haven't looked at that. We, we worry very much about what we are collecting and we, uh, treasury forecast is what we judge ourselves against.

But I imagine if you look around the world, it'll be a mixed picture and, uh, some economies in a number of economies globally have had, um, slow code, not, not so much the US probably, but if it helps China, it's a much slower economy. Perhaps if I could add state that commentary on Could you Yeah, sorry.

Just come forward to so that we can hear it through microphones. Um, treasury would consider the impact of changes to our p volumes and values, um, in comparison with other countries, and that would include that commentary when they do the, uh, economic forecast. So it's a, it's an answer, a, a question that could be answered by treasury officials.

Um, we would provide advice to them on what we are seeing. We are seeing, as we have said, uh, changes and the volumes of, um, high value imports and that's impacting our GST tank. But treasury would do the analysis of whether there are broader economic factors, including whether other countries slowdowns or import changes are, uh, different to what we are seeing.

Supplementary question. Francisco Hernandez. Uh, thank you minister. Thank you chair. Um, it's interesting that there's been, uh, while there's been a decline in the sort of trade volumes, um, according to annual report, there's been, uh, a big increase in the, uh, air travel and passenger cruise numbers coming to New Zealand.

Um, how do you explain that sort of countercyclical thing in terms of the opposite movement? Is it just because the borders have opened again? Like Basically, but actually, uh, air travel still hasn't fully recovered Right. From the pre pandemic levels. So we still running around 93% of the 2019 figures. Uh, cruise volumes came back strongly last year, but we're seeing lower of volumes this year and we are expecting to see lower of volumes again next year.

Yeah. And Connor, Thank you very much. Um, does the decline in revenue, and I guess the squeeze on the budget, um, affect your ability to, um, identify, uh, track and seize, uh, illicit high value goods like drugs In terms of the ability to operationally respond?

Yeah. I mean, and I congratulate you for, for capturing three tons. Yeah. How many tons do you estimate does indeed come across the border illegally? Well, we do, we have the water testing results, which you, you'll be aware of, um, which we are seeing an increase in volume. We are still seizing more than is being used. Um, so we have seen the increase, but you know, from, from what we can see, there is an increasing volume, um, of wastewater results.

We haven't kind of got a handle on that in terms terms of our capacity to continue to police the operational side of the, um, the activity of customs isn't impacted by the revenue it's collecting. Can I just come the estimate of the volume of drugs consumed within the country?

Yeah. Because I guess there are a flow on effects to health services, um, social consequences, et cetera. What, what is the estimate if UFCs three tons is that 3%, 10%? It's incredibly difficult to know. Yeah. So we are fortunate to have wastewater statistics in this country.

Many of the jurisdictions we would compare ourselves to do not. So, you know, I continue that last month, uh, per week, New Zealand just consumed 34 kilos of methamphetamine. Um, like stats for cocaine are about five kilos a week in end, DMA about ecstasy, about the same. But extrapolating from that as to what went missing is a really difficult thing to do.

I, we certainly have see, um, see 3.7 kilos, about 3.7 tons of drugs this year, and overseas partners just over three tons of, and that's highest ever with the one exception of cocaine last year where we had a super bowl of three, four tons through the, um, operational hydro.

The gangs manage the drug supply, they will stop pile, they will release drugs into the community. We're seeing a price drop in methamphetamine. So whether that is contributing to the, um, greater use probably is whether the gangs are trying to move their patches, get a bit more of a market.

It's probably happening too, but it is really difficult to to know how much we we're missing. I guess in my mind, we are seizing still far more than is far, far more than is being used. But of course we are missing some. I think something else that's worth mentioning is seizures now are often very large.

So if you look back 20 years, 20 years ago, we seized one kilo of methamphetamines in the whole year, one kilo. Now I get stats every morning on my desk, what we seized the day before. And we, if we're not seizing between 10 and 20 kilos a day, I'm worried that's how the market has changed. And, uh, you may have seen in the media recently operation what was nearly 500 kis of less in one shipment that we discovered an excellent piece of customs for trade craft, good cooperation lease.

But if you miss one 500 kilo shipment, it's not like missing a 20 kilo shipment. So we are working incredibly hard, keep up with this. I'd also say this is a global trend. Again, this is what we've see all around the world. Supplementary, Laura, Just, just tos on. Thanks, Ms. Chair. Just tos onto that.

Um, given that the transnational organized crime is such a big focus, I acknowledge it's not just your role, it's other government agencies working together and sometimes other countries working together. So how has that changed since the last time we saw you? Um, and is there a bit of an update into the progress on how those relationships are Going? I, I think the, I mean, you know, there is, um, ongoing activity we have, um, in, in terms of additional investment and resource.

One of the, um, key initiatives is we've, um, um, got cabinet approval to establish a ministerial advisory group to really focus on how all of the different agencies that contribute to this solution can work better together, um, share information better and respond more cohesively. I think the customs and the police model has been a really good example of how well this works.

And, and that's not saying there aren't other agencies working in this space at the moment, um, but it's just how we operationalize that and, and do it better and understand more how we can be more efficient. So that's the first initiative. Mm-Hmm. The other thing is around customs, um, looking to expand its presence overseas. Mm-Hmm. Um, and the customs does a lot of work and, and engaging.

I I think we just, um, finished a course working in the Solomon Islands, um, training officer over there. Um, had a huge role in through Chaum, um, working with Fiji around the, um, ability to, you know, respond. So we, we do reach out proactively and, and, um, the comptroller and her team work very proactively in that space to build relationships.

And it is that knowing a person has been really, really effective. Great. Francisco and Andy, thank you Chair. Um, I'm really interested to kind of, uh, explore your answer about the, the drugs. Um, what's the most common entry point? Is it passengers trying to smuggle it in? Is it through the container ships?

What's, how, how's it getting in? Well, I, I think I can respond. It's everywhere, right? So we have, um, couriers a lot more frequently coming through bringing, um, suitcases. Yeah. Within that, that's, um, increased significantly. We have a significant issue around trusted insiders, so people working within ports, um, within airports that are facilitating.

Um, we have mail, um, lots of mail interceptions, which are constantly, which was one of the things that surprised me is how much is posted, um, how much is intercepted through that process. Um, you couriers, um, and then large shipments, and I think we announced recently there was a, a significant, um, find that was, um, in steel beams in a, in a container.

So the, their agility and their ability to respond. Um, we have through the ports we have, um, um, sea chest where they, they're attached to ships without the ship even knowing it's there. Just everything you can think of if there's a way of getting into this country. Unfortunately, the legitimate supply chains usually support, um, the, you know, the ability to bring in, um, the, the stuff that we don't want in this country.

So yeah, pretty much it, it's a very nimble, um, agile environment and where there's a weak link, they'll find a way through. So that's, um, why the increased investment. Just recently this year, um, customs set up a, a investigative team in, um, Targa because of the level of activity at Targa Port.

So these are the sort of initiatives where, where there's a issue, they will respond and work collectively to, um, close the gap. And I've got up on That. Is it just, um, the drugs they're bringing in illegally through his roots, or are they bringing in things like firearms and other contraband as Well? Less firearms. There isn't. So there is generally there's been seizures where they have found drugs with firearms within it.

Um, but it's not, uh, to that level of volume. Um, it's, um, not something that they're, um, seizing as such, but drugs significantly and a significant amount of tobacco. Okay. Genius Lewis. Um, how many transnational organized, um, crime groups were disrupted in this and dismantled in this, um, annual, um, fiscal year?
And are the prosecution rates aligned with the seizure rates? Um, I might get Jamie to ask excuse.

Thank you. Uh, I can't give you an honest answer about how many, um, transnational organized crime groups have been disrupted because we don't actually count that. Um, but there are investigations under every day. We are frequently making arrests supporting police to make their arrests. Those are transnational organized crime groups.

Um, so as the minister mentioned recently, the, um, operation Good are the still beams that is, uh, obviously linked to, um, criminal elements in South America, the United States, uh, and, uh, um, elements here that is a transnational organized crime group. So, uh, those arrests and investigations go on every single day.

There are more importations than there are prosecutions. 'cause as we've mentioned before, the scale of, uh, of the, of the drug importations into the country is, is huge. And that is a worldwide trend, and we focus our investigations where we think we can have the most impact on the organized crime groups. So they're getting really sophisticated, um, these organized crime groups.

How, and what strategies are you using to ensure that you are either matching or outsmarting them? I think that secrets the biggest strength we have is with those international relationships and the, the way in which we're gathering intelligence. We're doing a lot more profiling and analysis, um, offshore.

So we're doing a lot more before people arrive. That's why we're doing so much more interceptions before they arrive here because of those relationships. Our intelligence systems are, are really good, but also looking at other technologies and equipments that we can use to improve our detection. Um, for example is, um, piece of work around the data for mail around analyzing mail before it comes here.

So we've got more information about what to target. Um, and the same thing with travel. Um, looking at, um, profiling, people profiling, um, trade to identify higher risk. So all of that intelligence analysis stuff is probably the biggest investment. We, I just have one follow up question about the revenue, um, question I asked before.

How does the government plan to address the implications of the 1.2 billion shortfall in crown revenue? And does this decrease jeopardize, um, funding public service? So the, and that's where I, I would highlight the trade agreements that have been developed and are in tranche at the moment around making, operationalizing those trade agreements.

And I think we've seen, um, a number of announcements around those trade agreements in the work that's been progressed in that space. Um, more recently the a CN agreement, um, in terms of the next stage of, um, facilitating those trade opportunities. So I think that's the, um, the biggest focus we have. The other component is, um, the customs, um, looking to appoint a liaison position in New Delhi to build those relationships in India.

So though all of those sort of pieces of work is, um, responding to, so when we have overseas postings, it's not just about, um, the, um, enforcement side of things, it's also to facilitate trade. So there's been some proactive work in that space as well. Thank you. Uh, just on the facilitating trade and, and I know that the figure internationally is very low.

That is, uh, paper-based transactions are still at the heart of most things. What, what's the, um, situation regarding our systems and then the interoperability with other systems and, and what are the hurdles to kind of improving that? I suppose it's single trade window, Sorry. Uh, but, you know, trade single window, we have a highly, highly automated, uh, very, very good system.

We are fortunate and the community of my colleagues who do not have such thing, for example, Australia doesn't, uh, used by both customs posted by customs, but also all EPI entries go through to, um, I, I just, I just feel grateful every day that we have it actually, because it enables us to do a lot of what the minister is talking about.

It enables us to pre-assess, uh, or imports, addiction exports, uh, well before they get here and, um, make plans. We think we're going to need to intervene. Um, it's not the case around the world. You're quite right. Uh, digital trade is not the norm. Uh, it's improving every year.

What do we try, try to do a range of things. Um, we are very active in the World Customs Organization, which is a multilateral, uh, group thing of customs administrations from 186 countries. Uh, Richard has chaired the safe framework for the WCA for the last two years. And if that's about facilitating efficient and effective and safe trade, so trying to help some of the countries which are willing to digitize their trade.

Uh, we are very involved in the apex, the subcommittee on customs procedures, and a lot of that is about promoting digitized trade. Uh, again, none of those economies, uh, very much wanting to digitize, but having to get themselves into the right place. Uh, I signed a, brings, uh, Singapore in the last few months, which again is looking to facilitate efficient trade and Singapore is highly digitized, so, um, I'm interested in learning what we came from them.

I think our systems are very good. The Singapore ian system reaches further into, uh, shipping companies for example, than we do here. It might might be a bit of a social license question about that, but yeah, that's an interesting thing that they're doing to get even more information that they could assess before. Good to us. And if I could give a little plug to optimize, you know, automating things as the traveler declaration, if you haven't got the app on your phone, please ensure next time you air travel that you do the online traveler declaration. Yes.

Yeah, I, I have very good actually. Great, thank you. And the reduced cues that customers are fabulous. Thank you. Is that providing direct benefit, do you think, in terms of helping to identify potentially some of the risks around Yeah, significantly. Both from an NPI perspective and, uh, a traveler perspective, it's really useful, um, to, as we've said, that pre um, analysis of who's coming into the country.

The, the earlier that we know that information, the better we are at targeting. So yeah, that, that, those are all those components around getting a bit smarter around what, what we're doing and being able to, um, know information earlier is really useful. And if I may say so, I think it's good for travelers because it gives them a bit of an indication as to what we're worried about and interested in, uh, somewhat in our space.

So for example, if you're bringing more than 10,000 bucks vacation to the country, that can, that's generally fine, but we need to know. And so this tells me you really have to do that from an NPI perspective. Uh, it gives, uh, Trevor an opportunity to understand that, for example, we won't let partner into the country so they can make a choice about whether they bring it and try to get it through or they just don't break the, which was fast form preference.

Can I just quickly supple on that? Yes. Um, are those forms available in other languages? Yes. Okay, good. Just 'cause I noticed that, you know, when you come in, there's sometimes people that are struggling to do The paper forms. Yes. We have a few languages, which the forms are in, and they have, um, a set of other, quite, quite a number, I think about 20 languages, which are not, forms, not in those, but there are helpful instructions.

Okay. Lisa, um, Melissa, can you just explain, um, what internal risk assessments were conducted regarding the teacher liabilities from elicit tobacco cases and why these assessments failed to predict the 9.9 million, um, liability that had to be paid?

Oh, so yeah, I think I might get the, um, comp controller so that that is the, um, The right, right off Off the debt. Yeah. So, well, it's a smuggled commodity, uh, and we don't know when we're going to get smuggling occurring, uh, that the tobacco was seized. Uh, and under our legislation, we actually have to assess the smuggled goods for, um, revenue, which I have to say feels a bit ridiculous to me because there was no way the smuggler are going to pay the revenue.

Yeah. Uh, and that happened very late in the financial year. And, and so we had to assess the revenue $9 million. Obviously we were never going to receive that on the path of the crown, so we have to write that off. And so that, that coming later, bless, see blew up helpful debt provision. We let, uh, we let those who need to know about that as quickly as we could.

Right. So at what point or did you ask for advice from either t um, or other organizations? This was done entirely with treasuries. Okay. Um, the treasury were totally informed and totally around forward budget. So are there any further steps that customs could take to address and or avoid this equation in the future? I feel there's A very converse decision here, which is Yeah, don't seize, Don't seize. o on the understanding because that, that's what creates the issue. Mm-Hmm. And the, the, the seizure late in the year was the big issue. So perhaps there's a process issue around how we manage that. Um, but at this point, this is the process that we're with and they have to evaluate, um, what is, you know, the, the revenue loss.

I, I actually think it's having to account for the revenue Mm-Hmm. Role. And that's something we will be talking to minister about climate Change. than the other way around. And obviously it's helpful knowing the potential value of what's coming in, but yeah, obviously there's an issue there. Yeah. From that perspective. And from a prioritization point of view, it's, you know, from a, you know, yes there was a accounting issue, but you know, it was easily explained. It was all compliant with, um, the processes. So I ask. Very good. Yeah. Do we have time for a quick question? Yep. Final Question. Final question. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, so my final question would be at, um, the, both the budget and I think the previous annual review we discussed, um, some of the health and safety around, uh, because of the increased, um, risk with some of the

seizures and things that you're doing. Um, has there been any operational changes, any additional training given? And do you see there being another budget bid to increase that part next time around and without foresee the future too much, just given the trends are going up? Yeah. Yeah, yeah. From a personal perspective, I think there is a real need, particularly the types of searches and, you know Yeah. Brought in is, is the other issue. Mm-Hmm. So yeah, I would say there's a lot more the, the, the key one where I see, um, some real issues is around our drug storage and disposal processes. Mm-Hmm. We are getting massive volumes and the way in which we, um, deal with that, you know, it's representing high risk.

So I think we've got to look at some stuff in that space as well, and we're doing some work in that. Great. Thanks. Right. I just would add, we, we look at our equipment and training all the time and we've just agreed to spend about $300,000 on, um, recutting out our maritime teams because we've fallen behind.

And so we'll bring ourselves up to the same level as the Navy and Australian Defense Force. So, so you, those decisions that we take are very staff who go out on either drug jobs for child exploitation jobs now, not just, but holistic, which is really unfortunate.

Uh, but also to be clear, the police are in, you know, an absolutely dispensable part of, um, our observations in those spaces. Richard Barge, who's Deputy Chief Executive Policy Legal and Strategy. Cheryl May, deputy Chief Executive Enterprise Services, Joe Hanon, chief Executive International Governance, Greg Hanon, chief Financial Officer, and David. So, uh, chief Legal Officer. Um, I think we've talked quite a lot about our protection role, so, uh, don't necessarily need to go through that again, but I've actually got some statistics just for this year, which, which are still very much on the same trajectory as we've been talking about.

So, uh, from January to October we have seized two, two tons of methamphetamine, uh, nearly 600 kilos of powdered MDMA and over 400 kilograms of cocaine. Um, sorry I missed that last one. I'm sorry. 400 kilos of cocaine. Uh, and they're overseas partners. Uh, one point, uh, sorry, 1.9 tons of meth, uh, 200 kilos of cocaine and 130 kilos.

VDA has stopped for us, uh, although destined for New Zealand. So this is very sad and obvious trend. Um, and as you've heard, the transnational organized crime groups are continuing to be agile. They are adaptive, they are sophisticated, and they have a lot of money, which, uh, means that sort of, um, smuggling means we are seeing, uh, extremely, uh, capable, I guess, uh, it, it is quite common now to see extremely expensive, uh, large machinery imported, uh, with very cunning concealments of drugs.

And the gangs are quite prepared to spend the money on that machinery. Do the concealment ship the drugs here? Ironically, they do pay the import duties and then, um, and then we seize them. Um, we do work very closely, as the minister said, with partner agencies in New Zealand and overseas to provide our resources to target and disrupt these supply chains, the criminals and their profits.
